title: Inflation Calculator (purchasing power over time)
shortDescription: See how inflation erodes purchasing power: future cost of today's money, plus today's value of a future amount.
description: Enter an amount, time horizon, and expected inflation rate. The calculator returns both directions: how much you'll need in the future to maintain today's purchasing power, and the present value of a future amount.
metaDescription: Free inflation calculator. Convert money across time: future cost of today's amount, present value of future amount, lost purchasing power.
empty: Enter amount, years, and inflation rate.

result.futureNote: What you'll need in N years to buy today's basket of goods.
result.presentNote: What that amount in N years is worth in today's money.

sections.heading: Why inflation matters for long-term planning
paragraphs: Inflation reduces the purchasing power of money over time. At 2% inflation, $1,000 today buys what $610 buys in 25 years — almost half. At 5% inflation the erosion is dramatic: same $1,000 buys what $295 buys after 25 years. Even 'low' inflation compounds invisibly over decades.

This is why holding cash long-term is a guaranteed loss in real terms unless interest rates exceed inflation. Savings accounts paying 0.5% lose 1.5% per year against 2% inflation. Investments need to outpace inflation just to break even.

sections.heading: Realistic inflation rates by era and country
paragraphs: Long-term US average (since 1913): about 3.2%. Recent decades have been lower (2010s averaged 1.7%) but 2021-2024 spiked to 4-9% in many countries. Most central banks target 2% as 'price stability'.

Japan: famously low (~0% average for 1990-2020) but recently picked up to 2-3%. Eurozone: 1-3% historically, but 6-10% in 2022. UK: similar to Eurozone. Emerging markets (Turkey, Argentina): often double-digit, sometimes hyperinflation.
For long-horizon planning (10+ years), assume 2-3% nominal in stable economies as a baseline. Stress-test with 4-5% to see how a higher-inflation scenario affects you.

sections.heading: Two formulas, two directions
paragraphs: Future cost: FV = PV × (1 + i)^n. To maintain $50,000/year living costs at 2% inflation for 30 years, you'll need $90,568/year then. This is the budget-planning view: how much income or balance you need at a future date.

Present value: PV = FV / (1 + i)^n. Receiving $1M in 25 years at 2.5% inflation is worth about $539,000 today. This is the comparison view: when comparing future cash flows (lottery payouts, pension lump sums, retirement accounts), discount to present value to compare apples to apples.
Compound interest grows money exponentially; inflation shrinks it exponentially. For investments to truly grow, you need a real rate (nominal − inflation) > 0.

howTo.name: Enter the amount
howTo.text: Either today's amount or a future amount.

howTo.name: Enter the time horizon
howTo.text: Number of years between today and the future date.

howTo.name: Read both directions
howTo.text: Future cost of today's amount, and today's value of a future amount.

faq.q: What inflation rate should I use?
faq.a: For US/EU/JP planning: 2-3% nominal. For stress testing: 4-5%. For currently-volatile periods, the central bank's recent CPI report is most relevant.
Consumer Price Index — a basket of goods/services that governments track to measure inflation. 'Inflation rate' is usually the year-over-year change in CPI.

faq.q: Does the calculator account for personal inflation rate?
faq.a: No — it uses a single rate. Personal inflation varies by spending habits: rent and food inflate differently from electronics. For granular planning, weight by your actual budget.
Yes — enter a negative rate. Japan experienced this in the 2000s. Deflation increases purchasing power over time but is generally bad for the economy (people delay spending).

faq.q: How is this different from compound interest?
faq.a: Compound interest grows your money; inflation shrinks its purchasing power. They use the same exponential math but in opposite directions. For real returns, subtract inflation from your nominal investment rate.

faq.q: Should I use this for retirement planning?
faq.a: Yes — combine with our retirement-calculator to see both nominal and real (today-dollar) projections of your savings.

faq.q: Where do I find historical inflation data?
faq.a: US: BLS.gov. Japan: e-Stat (Statistics Bureau). EU: Eurostat. UK: ONS. World Bank also publishes per-country data going back decades.

faq.q: Does the data leave my browser?
faq.a: No. Calculation runs locally; nothing is sent to a server.
